narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid middle-income family background and plans to attend law school full-time, you've put real effort into your application, and I appreciate how challenging this process can feel. The admissions model we use predicted an 82.1% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is why your application for our law school program was unfortunately rejected—it's designed to prioritize students with the strongest projected odds of bar passage success. This prediction was driven primarily by your academic performance in school, especially your Year 3 grades, which placed you in the lowest decile compared to your peers, strongly pushing the model toward expecting a bar failure. Your Year 1 grades, also in the bottom decile, reinforced this concern as the second-biggest factor pulling the prediction in that direction. On a brighter note, your LSAT score of 35 stood out positively, as it was close to the typical student's score and helped nudge the model toward predicting you'd pass. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.90 played a smaller supportive role in the same way, showing some resilience despite being below average. Compared to typical students in our dataset—who often rank in the middle or higher deciles for school grades, score around 36.7 on the LSAT, and have GPAs near 3.21—your lower early rankings were the standout red flags that set you apart in a way the model couldn't overlook. Your middle family income and full-time enrollment plans had minimal sway, aligning closely with the norm where most applicants are full-time. While this outcome stings, it's not the end of your path to law—consider retaking courses or boosting your academic deciles through targeted improvement, perhaps seeking tutoring or additional undergrad-level prep to realign with stronger performers. You might also explore part-time programs or schools with more flexible admissions while building your credentials further. We're rooting for your success, and many students in similar spots have turned things around with persistence. Keep pushing forward—you've got this.
